trajectory_summary: The call opens cold — Jessica's first substantive line is a soft refusal — and Tyke recovers it into a booking through a reactive but functional curiosity pivot. The booking itself is real, but it is built on no qualification foundation: no verify section, no bill anchor, no co-decision-maker check, and a button-up that is missing at least four of six required structural elements. The call's shape is a cold-start recovery that lands a fragile win.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown' but the call ends with a booked appointment (next Saturday, time ambiguous between 12PM and 3PM per ASR noise at 01:53–01:57). The outcome should be tagged 'appointment_booked — fragile.' The booking is real but structurally vulnerable: no qualification was completed, the moving-in context was not explored, no co-decision-maker was identified, and the button-up is incomplete. This appointment carries elevated no-show and disqualification risk.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: We can do any day next week. What time would work best for you?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Tyke booked a sit with zero qualification data collected — no homeowner confirmation, no co-decision-maker check, no bill anchor. The moving-in disclosure at 01:19 compounds this: the closer will arrive at a customer who may not yet have utility history at the address and whose homeowner status was never confirmed. On a follow-up call where prior data may be stale, a 60-second verify pass after Jessica opened the door at 01:06 was both possible and necessary. This is the single gap most likely to produce a no-show or a disqualified sit.

key_moments.quote: Oh, is it for a good reason? You're you're not one of the reasons to take a look at these guys? I'm gonna right?
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke's response to the soft refusal is the call's most structurally important moment. The phrasing is garbled — likely ASR noise compressing a stumbling live delivery — but the underlying move is a curiosity probe consistent with the changed_mind rebuttal strategy: don't argue, create curiosity. The pivot works: Jessica re-engages at 00:41. Strategic read holds over reactive read here because the outcome is recovery, not capitulation. However, the entry into the pivot is rough enough that it could easily have landed as pressure rather than curiosity on a more guarded customer.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I wanted to hear them out about the solar thing, about the free program.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jessica's use of 'the free program' is a direct signal that her mental model of what she signed up for is 'free solar' — not a design consultation with a purchase path. Tyke's response at 00:46 — 'Is it a free program or something like that? Yeah. No. There's no out of pocket cost for the upfront fees' — is ambiguous at best. 'No out of pocket cost for the upfront fees' does not correct the 'free program' framing; it partially validates it. The closer will walk into a customer who may believe she's receiving a free installation, not evaluating a purchase.

key_moments.quote: We're kinda busy next week just because we we're not even moved in yet. We're gonna be moving in next week.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a qualification risk signal, not an enthusiasm signal. Jessica discloses she is not yet living in the home — she is moving in next week. This raises at minimum two unresolved questions: (1) Is she the homeowner or a renter moving into a new rental? (2) Does she have any bill history for this property? Tyke's response — 'You're fine. We could do weekends as well' — routes entirely around the disclosure to preserve the booking. The booking is preserved, but the closer will arrive at a customer who may have no utility bill history at the address and whose homeowner status was never confirmed on this call.

key_moments.quote: Is it a free program or something like that? Yeah. No. There's no out of pocket cost for the upfront fees. We go over all with you. It's just a preliminary meeting. So if it doesn't pencil out how it should or if it doesn't make sense to you guys, then, yeah, we shake hands, walk away.
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke's 'no obligation' framing is a legitimate trust-building move — he's lowering the perceived risk of the appointment. The 'shake hands, walk away' line is effective. However, the framing is entirely about what the customer loses nothing, not about what they gain. There is no bill anchor, no curiosity creation, no 'here's what you'll actually see' hook. The micro-trade lands as permission to disengage rather than as a reason to show up.
